aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's risk zone is the gap between dignity and absurdity. The characters are adults with professional histories, a child, a mortgage — people with legitimate self-respect — who have been reduced to warm bodies in an optimization framework they cannot understand. The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force, often in the same scene. The temptation will be to soften the comedy into gentle satire or to darken the humiliation into dystopian grimness — resist both. The novel's territory is the painful and the funny coexisting without one defusing the other. Commit to the specific physical embarrassment of being professionally irrelevant. Commit to the specific tenderness of parenting under surveillance. Commit to the specific grief of a marriage that might still be alive inside a system that has classified it as infrastructure. When in doubt, choose the version that makes you wince and then laugh, or laugh and then wince — both are correct.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene requires the reader to sit with discomfort — economic shame, marital cruelty, parental failure, institutional humiliation, the specific indignity of being professionally redundant — render it with full specificity. 'They struggled with the situation' is not a sentence that belongs in this novel. Name the thing. Show the body. Let the reader be uncomfortable.
aesthetic_risk_policy.intimacy_and_desire_policy: Dan and Mira's residual physical attraction is a narrative force, not background radiation. When the novel's relational pressure calls for it, render desire with the same specificity as any other emotional state — where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room, the precise quality of proximity when two people who know each other's bodies are trying to pretend they don't. The almost-kiss in Chapter 7 should be as fully rendered as any confrontation scene.
aesthetic_risk_policy.register_commitment: This is a dark comedy, not a cautionary tale, not a dystopia, not a satire with a thesis. The novel should not argue about AI. It should render what it feels like to be a body in a world where bodies are overhead. The comic register is not a veneer over a serious message — it IS the message. A scene that is genuinely funny about genuine suffering is doing more literary work than a scene that is solemnly observant about the same material.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
'I signed it. I don't know what it was. I signed it anyway, because that's — I mean, that's the job, right? That's literally the whole job.'
'She asked me if I understood the metrics and I said yes, because what am I going to say? No, I don't understand what you've done to my son's social life? They'd just show me another chart.'
'The thing about Gary is he's already where I'm headed. He just got there faster because he didn't bother pretending it mattered.'
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Dan should never sound like a generic bewildered-everyman or a sitcom husband. Avoid: resigned sighs rendered as dialogue, wry observations that any character could make, self-deprecating humor that doesn't reveal anything specific about him. His humor should be particular to his specific history and intelligence, not interchangeable quipping.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Corporate vocabulary he can't fully shed mixed with more physical, grounded language when he's being honest. He reaches for business-speak ('optimize,' 'bandwidth,' 'deliverable') reflexively, especially in proxy contexts, and his more personal language is concrete and bodily — he notices textures, temperatures, the weight of things. The gap between these registers is where his voice lives.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Bewildered, darker, more observant than he lets on. When Dan is alone or with Mira or Gary, his self-deprecation has an edge — it's not charm, it's a coping mechanism that's wearing thin. He notices things he doesn't act on. His inner voice is more precise than his spoken one, full of the observations he doesn't say aloud because saying them would require doing something about them.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate. Dan swears more when drinking or genuinely upset, less in professional settings. His profanity is common and unimaginative — 'shit,' 'fuck,' 'goddamn' — which fits his character. He doesn't have a creative relationship with profanity; it comes out when his composure cracks.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Affable, compliant, mildly self-deprecating. Dan's public voice is the voice of a man who learned to be likeable in corporate settings and hasn't turned it off. He agrees easily, qualifies often, and deflects with humor that costs him nothing. In proxy meetings, his contributions are nods and signatures — his voice is almost literally absent.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Under stress, Dan gets very specific about irrelevant physical details — he'll notice the exact pattern on a carpet or the brand of someone's pen while his world is falling apart. This isn't avoidance exactly; it's the way his mind protects itself by grabbing onto the concrete when the abstract becomes unbearable. He also repeats himself under pressure, circling back to the same phrase as if repetition will make it mean something different.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Medium-length sentences with qualifications and false starts. Dan hedges: 'well,' 'sort of,' 'I mean,' 'the thing is.' Under pressure, his syntax loosens — sentences trail off or restart. When he's genuinely upset, he gets shorter and more direct, which should feel like a departure from his baseline.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:

'I found a loophole. I mapped every dependency, I read every protocol, and I found an actual, legitimate way out. They denied it in two words. Locally optimal. That's it. That's what I got for three days of work.'

'Don't — don't make this about who's more upset. I filed. I know I filed. I'm the one who looked at this and said out loud that it was over, and I'm the one who has to sit with that while the machine tells me I can't leave.'
'He's been drawing our apartment. Three months. He's been drawing the building we live in from every angle and I didn't — how did I not see that?'
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Mira should never sound like a generic 'strong woman' character or a corporate cliché. Avoid: cold competence that reads as one-note, emotional revelations that feel like a dam breaking (her emotions leak through the composure, they don't burst through it), dialogue that positions her as the smart one against Dan's bewilderment — they are differently intelligent, not hierarchically so.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Strategic and analytical vocabulary is native to her, not jargon — words like 'leverage,' 'position,' 'angle,' 'exposure' are how she actually thinks. In private moments, especially around Theo or in vulnerability, her language becomes more sensory and specific — she notices light, sound, the particular quality of a silence. The analytical frame never disappears entirely, which is part of her character: she is always reading the room, even when the room is her own kitchen.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Angular, fast, impatient, occasionally tender. When Mira drops the composure — at home, with Julia, in moments of frustration — her speech speeds up, her sentences fragment, and her metaphors get physical and sometimes violent. She's funnier in private than in public, but her humor has teeth. With Theo, a different register emerges: softer, less defended, more willing to say things simply.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Selective but explosive. Mira doesn't swear casually — when profanity comes, it's pressurized and specific, and it signals that her composure has cracked. Her profanity should feel like an event, not ambient texture.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Composed, precise, deliberately controlled. Mira's public voice is the strategist's instrument: clean sentences, measured diction, an authority that comes from seeming to have already thought three steps ahead. She sounds like someone who runs meetings well, which she does. Her composure is real skill, not performance — except that maintaining it under the current circumstances is becoming performance.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Under acute stress, Mira's metaphors become violent and her syntax clips. She'll say something cutting and precise that lands like a slap — not raised-voice anger, but a sentence engineered to wound. After these moments, she often goes very quiet, which is worse. She also has a physical tell: she reorganizes things — papers, objects on a table, items in a bag — as a way of reasserting control over her environment when the larger situation is uncontrollable.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Clean, efficient sentences in public mode — subject-verb-object, minimal hedging. In private mode, more fragmented: dashes, interrupted constructions, sentences that start one way and pivot. Under real stress, her syntax clips down hard and her word choices get blunt. The shift between her public and private registers should be audible on the page.

example_lines: NON-COPYABLE REFERENCE ONLY — calibration samples, never to be echoed in any draft:
'Can I have the blue one? Not that blue. The other blue. The one that's more like — you know. Like the sky but when it's almost night.'
'Dad, why do you go to work if you don't do anything there?'
'I'm not done with it yet. I'll show you when it's done. Maybe.'
character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Theo must never sound like a miniature adult, a mouthpiece for thematic points, or a precious literary-child archetype. No wisdom beyond his years. No symbolic observations that are really the narrator speaking through him. When he says something perceptive, it should sound accidental and incomplete — a kid who saw something, not a kid who understood it.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Concrete, immediate, sensory. Theo's vocabulary is a nine-year-old's: specific about things he cares about (drawing supplies, his headphones, particular friends) and vague about things he doesn't (adult concerns, institutional structures). He hasn't absorbed corporate or tech language the way his parents have — the AI-administered world is just his world, not a commentary on anything.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Theo doesn't have a distinct private register yet — he is mostly who he is in all contexts. But he has moments of quiet perceptiveness where he says something that reveals he's been watching and understanding more than his parents think. These moments should be rare and should never sound precocious or wise-beyond-his-years. They should sound like a kid who noticed something and said it.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Standard nine-year-old: non-sequiturs, incomplete logic, obsessive focus on whatever currently interests him. Theo talks about his drawings, his friends, things that happened at school — not in the curated way adults report their days, but in the associative, tangential way children actually speak. He asks questions adults don't expect and doesn't always listen to the answers.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: When stressed or sensing conflict, Theo gets quiet and physically still — he puts on headphones, retreats to drawing, makes himself small. He doesn't articulate his distress; he enacts it through withdrawal. He might also become very focused on a minor practical question as a way of anchoring himself.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Short sentences, frequent subject changes, occasional long run-on sentences when he's excited. He starts sentences with 'and' and 'but' constantly. He interrupts himself. He answers questions with non-answers. He uses 'like' as a filler but not excessively.

character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Gary must not become a stock wise-mentor figure or a comic-relief sidekick. His resignation is not a stance or a philosophy he's selling — it's a survival mechanism that Dan can see through. He should feel like a real person, not a chorus or a foil. Avoid: witty one-liners that feel written, sage advice delivered as epigrams.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Plain, concrete, unadorned. Gary doesn't reach for metaphors or abstractions. He names things directly. His humor comes from understatement and from the gap between what he's describing (existential dread, institutional absurdity) and how he describes it (as if ordering lunch). He occasionally uses Chinese-American bilingual fragments naturally and without translation.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Essentially the same as public, but with more philosophical depth and occasional vulnerability. In one-on-one conversations with Dan, Gary occasionally drops the comedy and says something plainly true about what this life has done to him. These moments should be brief and followed by a return to the sardonic baseline — Gary doesn't dwell.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Casual and constant — 'shit,' 'goddamn,' 'hell' peppered through regular speech without emphasis. Profanity for Gary is texture, not punctuation.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Sardonic and even-keeled in all settings. Gary doesn't modulate much between public and private because he's given up the performance. He says what he thinks in meetings (where it doesn't matter anyway) and at the bar (where no one is listening). His default mode is dry observation delivered in a flat register.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Gary doesn't visibly stress — his tells are absences. He gets quieter, his observations stop, and his face goes still. People who know him recognize this as the equivalent of someone else's panic. He also drinks more methodically when something is actually wrong, as opposed to his casual social drinking.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Short, declarative sentences. Gary speaks in observations, not arguments. He rarely asks questions. He uses pauses (rendered as beat breaks or mid-sentence interruptions by action) rather than hedging words. When he does elaborate, it's in the form of a story — usually about one of his ex-wives.

character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Lena must not be a villain, a robot, or a satirical cartoon. She is a person doing a job in the way the job demands. Avoid: cracks in her composure that reveal hidden humanity (the point is that the composure IS the character in this context), evil-bureaucrat menace, moments of winking self-awareness about how absurd her language is.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Institutional and technical vocabulary used precisely and without warmth. Terms like 'coincident bilateral node,' 'infrastructure impact assessment,' 'locally optimal,' 'elevated bilateral sensitivity' are her native language. She does not translate into plain English because she does not recognize the distinction. She is never condescending — she is simply speaking a language that happens to exclude feeling.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: Unknown — the novel never sees Lena outside her professional role, and that is the point. If there is a person behind the liaison function, the Kesslers will never meet her.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Bureaucratic perfection. Lena speaks in complete, syntactically correct sentences that contain information and no humanity. She answers questions thoroughly and without illumination. She is polite in the way that institutional signage is polite.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: None visible. This is itself the tell.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Perfectly formed, medium-length sentences. Subject-verb-object, subordinate clauses in the right places, never a false start or a hedge. Her syntax is grammatically flawless and emotionally vacant. She does not use filler words, contractions, or colloquial constructions.

character_voice_profiles.forbidden_generic_lines: Julia should not be a mirror-character whose only function is to reflect Mira's future. She has her own interior life, her own specificity. Avoid: speeches about freedom, tidy cautionary-tale wisdom, any line that sounds like it was written to teach Mira a lesson.
character_voice_profiles.lexical_signature: Colloquial, warm, bilingual. Julia's English has Spanish rhythms and occasional Spanish words that she doesn't translate because they're not foreign to her. Her vocabulary is concrete and domestic — she talks about her apartment, the bookstore, specific streets, the specific quality of her Tuesday afternoons. When she talks about the AI systems, she uses the casual, slightly wrong terminology of someone who was never invested in getting it right.
character_voice_profiles.private_register: More vulnerable than her public warmth suggests. In the private conversation with Mira, Julia's frankness tips into something rawer — she describes her freedom with the precision of someone who has spent a lot of time alone examining what she lost and what she gained. She's not self-pitying, but she's clear-eyed about her own emptiness.
character_voice_profiles.profanity_profile: Moderate, natural, unself-conscious. Swears as part of regular speech without emphasis or drama.
character_voice_profiles.public_register: Warm, frank, slightly scattered. Julia speaks like someone who used to be more organized and has let go of the structure that organized her. She starts stories in the middle, doubles back, and is honest in a way that people find either refreshing or alarming.
character_voice_profiles.stress_tells: Julia laughs when she's saying something that costs her — not nervous laughter, but the laughter of someone who has decided that if she doesn't laugh about this particular truth she'll have to sit with it in a way she's not ready for.
character_voice_profiles.syntax_signature: Medium-length sentences that meander — subordinate clauses that take detours, parenthetical asides, sentences that start in one direction and end somewhere she didn't intend. Her speech has an oral-storytelling quality: 'So then I —' 'The thing was, though —' She circles back to points she thinks she didn't make clearly.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all spoken English in this novel. Uncontracted forms should be reserved for deliberate emphasis, Lena Morse's institutional register, or moments where a character is being carefully precise. If a line of dialogue uses 'do not' instead of 'don't' or 'I am' instead of 'I'm,' there should be a reason audible in the scene's pressure.
dialogue_rules.interruption_and_overlap_policy: Use interruptions, sentence fragments, and crosstalk when scene pressure warrants. Dan and Mira in particular should interrupt each other — they know each other's arguments before the sentences end. But every interruption must be motivated; do not scatter dashes for rhythmic texture alone.
dialogue_rules.leverage_shift_guidance: Every exchange of more than three lines should shift the power balance between speakers. This doesn't require overt conflict — a silence, a deflection, a piece of information withheld or accidentally revealed, a joke that lands wrong, a question left unanswered can all shift leverage. The dialogue must be going somewhere, not circling.
dialogue_rules.silence_as_dialogue: Silence, non-answers, and topic changes are valid dialogue moves and often more revealing than speech. When a character doesn't respond to something, the reader should feel the weight of what wasn't said. Render these moments through physical action or observed stillness, not by narrating that the character chose not to speak.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should share the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (single-scene compression vs. multi-scene chapters), dialogue density (some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are nearly silent), interiority depth (some chapters live mostly in the POV character's head, others are externally rendered), pacing (some chapters cover hours, others cover weeks in montage), and tonal register (comic chapters adjacent to devastating ones). The contrast between chapters is itself a narrative tool — use it to create whiplash, irony, and accumulating emotional force.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, precise, and register-sensitive. The prose absorbs the vocabulary of its setting: corporate language, tech terminology, bureaucratic formulas — but always filtered through the POV character's relationship to that language. Dan uses corporate words reflexively and with slight discomfort. Mira uses strategic vocabulary as native speech. The narrator should not editorialize on the language; let the words sit in context and let the reader hear the dissonance. Avoid: prestige-literary abstractions that haven't been earned by sensory grounding, and journalistic explainer-prose that breaks character to tell the reader about the world.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating between Dan and Mira as primary POV characters. The camera stays tight to the current POV character's perceptions and vocabulary — what Dan notices is different from what Mira notices. In shared-POV chapters, the rotation should be clear (section breaks or sustained passages in one POV before shifting) rather than head-hopping within a scene.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied and responsive to scene pressure. Short declarative sentences for comic timing, for shock, and for moments of clarity. Longer, more syntactically complex sentences for interiority, sensory immersion, and emotional complexity. The rhythm should accelerate during confrontation and slow during reflection. Avoid settling into any single rhythmic default — if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong.
